cue: Backtracking | String Builder ⟩T: Backtracking to generate all balanced parens; the wrong instinct is pruning only on total count and forgetting the ordering rule — a ) is illegal whenever closes woul

cue: Backtracking | Include-Exclude ⟩T: Generate every subset; the pick/skip recursion is the clean pattern, and the easy-to-forget step is undoing the add (temp.remove) between the two recursive branches
oneLiner: Recursively build subsets by making a "take it" choice (add the element, explore, then erase it) and a "leave it" choice (just explore) at each index, ensuring you save a deep copy of the shared list when you reach the end of the array.

Recursively build permutations by looping through the original array, using a boolean "visited" array to skip already-used numbers, and applying the "add, recurse, remove" pattern to explore all sequences.

Recursively branch the 1-step choice before the 2-step choice to guarantee lexicographical order, making sure to undo the choice after exploring and saving a deep copy of the path when the remaining steps hit zero

Recursively traverse the grid by exploring the Down ('D') branch before the Right ('R') branch to guarantee lexicographical order, using string concatenation to implicitly manage the backtracking state until reaching the bottom-right corner

To find the A-th largest element, first pad the answer array with A-1 negative ones and build an initial Min Heap using the first A elements. Then, iterate the rest of the array: if the incoming element is greater than heap.peek(), poll() the root and add the new element, appending heap.peek() to your answer array at every step

cue: Matrix Traversal | Diagonal ⟩T: "Print/collect all anti-diagonals of a matrix" → need to group elements where row + col is constant ⟩M: Anti-diagonals share the invariant r + c = constant. Start from
oneLiner: To find all anti-diagonals, trace from two sets of starting points: the top row (col 0 to N-1) and the rightmost column (row 1 to N-1). From each starting point (r, c), collect elements by moving down-left (r+1, c-1) and place them into the current row of the output matrix. Java's default initialization handles the trailing zeros automatically

To set matrix zeroes in O(1) space, use Row 0 and Col 0 as flag arrays. First, check if Row 0 or Col 0 inherently contain zeros and store this in two booleans. Next, iterate from i=1, j=1; if A[i][j]==0, set A[i][0]=0 and A[0][j]=0. Iterate from 1,1 again and apply the zeros based on the flags. Finally, update Row 0 and Col 0 based on the saved booleans

To check if two trees are identical, do not flatten them into arrays. Use simultaneous recursion: if both nodes are null, return 1; if only one is null or values differ, return 0. Recursively check if isSameTree(A.left, B.left) AND isSameTree(A.right, B.right) both return 1

Use a HashMap to store the last_seen_index of each element. Execution: Iterate through the array. If the current element exists in the map, calculate the distance: current_index - map.get(element), and update the global minimum distance. Regardless of whether it was found or not, always update the map with the current_index so future matches use the closest possible index. If the global minimum is never updated, return -1

Extract all start times into one array and end times into another. Sort both arrays. Use two pointers (i for start, j for end). Execution: > 1. The Core Relation: To reach step N, you either came from step N-1 or N-2. Therefore: ways(N) = (ways(N-1) + ways(N-2)) % 1000000007. 2. Top-Down (Memoization): Init array with -1. Base cases: if (N == 0 || N == 1) return 1;. Check cache: if (dp[N] != -1) return dp[N];. Recurse, modulo, and save. (Time: O(N), Space: O(N)). 3. Bottom-Up (Tabulation): Base cases dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1. Loop i = 2 to N: dp[i] = (dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]) % 1000000007. 4. Senior Optimization (Slider): We only need the last two steps. Use a=1, b=1. Loop from 2 to N: c = (a + b) % 1000000007, then shift a = b and b = c. Return c. (Time: O(N), Space: O(1))
Find Anti diagonal sum

Fix the middle element j, then count all strictly smaller elements to its left (left_smaller) and strictly greater elements to its right (right_larger). For each $j$, the number of valid triplets is simply left_smaller * right_larger, bringing the O(N^3) brute force down to an optimal O(N^2) time complexity

Convert the integer to a string and use an $O(N^2)$ nested loop to generate all contiguous subarrays, maintaining a running product inside the inner loop. Check each running product against a HashSet, returning 0 immediately if a duplicate is found, or 1 if all loops finish successfully.

cue: DP | Catalan Number ⟩T: Count structurally unique BSTs (or any "count all valid structures from a smaller count of parts") → Catalan number DP, root-split recurrence ⟩M: Fix k as root → left subtree u
oneLiner: To find the total unique BSTs for i nodes, pick one node as the root. The remaining i-1 nodes must be split between the left and right subtrees. If j nodes go left, i-1-j nodes must go right. The total combinations for that specific root is the product of the subtrees: dp[j] * dp[i-1-j]. Sum this product for all possible root splits (j from 0 to i-1) to get dp[i]

The implementation is nearly identical to 0/1 Knapsack, but when you pick an item, you stay on the current row i instead of moving back to i-1 so the same item can be selected again. Calculate dp[i][j] as the maximum of either skipping the item completely (dp[i-1][j]) or taking it if capacity allows (value[i] + dp[i][j - weight[i]])

Treat the outer loop i as introducing a "new item" and the inner loop j as testing every possible "bag size," where the array index always represents the capacity. Read the transition equation as a physical choice for the current bag size j: you either skip the item and keep yesterday's best profit (prev[j]), or pack it by adding its value to yesterday's best profit for the leftover space (prev[j - weight])

cue: DFS | Path Visited Array ⟩T: Detect cycle in a directed graph → DFS with visited + pathVisited (recursion-stack) arrays ⟩M: For each unvisited node, DFS marking both visited[idx]=true (global, never u
oneLiner: Use two boolean arrays: visited (global memory to avoid redundant work) and pathVisited (active path memory to detect cycles). When entering a node, mark both as true; if you ever encounter a neighbor where pathVisited is already true, you have found a cycle! When backtracking out of a node, set pathVisited to false (picking up your breadcrumb), but leave visited as true to ensure you never process that node again.

cue: DFS ⟩T: eachability from source to target in a directed graph; the trap is 1-indexed nodes (size arrays/lists as A+1, not A) and treating a directed edge as bidirectional. Build adjacency list, DFS fr
oneLiner: To find if a path exists from node $X$ to node $Y$, you do not need complex return logic. Simply initialize a global visited array, run a standard void DFS starting exclusively from node $X$, and afterward, check if visited[Y] == true. If it is, a path exists.

cue: BFS | Dummy Node Trick ⟩T: Shortest path with only two distinct edge weights (e.g., 1 and 2) → BFS with a dummy intermediate node splitting the heavier edges, instead of Dijkstra ⟩M: For every weight-
oneLiner: To handle weight-2 edges, insert a dummy node in the middle of each to convert them into two weight-1 edges. Then, run a standard BFS from the source and return the distance the moment you reach the destination.

cue: Dijkstra | Min Heap ⟩T: Single-source shortest path, weighted graph (non-negative weights) → Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap ⟩M: Build adjacency list (both directions if undirected). Init dist[]
oneLiner: Use a Priority Queue (Min-Heap) storing (node, total_weight_from_source) and a distance array initialized to -1. Continuously pop the minimum weight node, and if it hasn't been visited, lock in its distance and push all its neighbors into the queue with their new cumulative weights.

Calculate the in-degree for all nodes and push those with an in-degree of 0 into a Queue (use a PriorityQueue if you need the lexicographically smallest order). Pop nodes, add them to your answer array, and decrement their neighbors' in-degrees—pushing any neighbor that drops to 0 into the queue—and finally check if the answer size matches the total nodes to ensure no cycles exist

exampleProblem: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stocks II
oneLiner: Don't manually track peaks and valleys. Use a Greedy approach: loop from index 1 to N, and anytime A[i] > A[i-1], simply add A[i] - A[i-1] to your total profit. Every upward daily step contributes to the total
Don't calculate the factorial (overflow). Count the pairs of (2, 5). 2s are abundant, so just count the 5s using a loop: count += n / i where i multiplies by 5 each step.
You cannot divide under a modulo! To compute (A / B) mod M, turn division into multiplication using Fermat's formula: (A * B^{M-2})mod M

If a problem asks if ANY valid state exists (e.g., Sudoku), return boolean from your recursive calls to instantly kill the search once found. If a problem asks to generate ALL valid states (e.g., N-Queens), keep the return type void. Let the recursion naturally unfold and save the state only inside your base case

cue: DP | HashSet ⟩T: Can a string be segmented into dictionary words → recursion + memoization on "can I break the suffix starting at index i" ⟩M: Define canBreak(i) = true if s[i:] can be fully segmented
oneLiner: The Architecture: Convert the dictionary to a HashSet for $O(1)$ lookups. Use a Boolean[] dp array of the same length as the string to store whether a breakdown is possible from a specific index. The Logic: Loop i from idx + 1 to A.length(). If dict.contains(A.substring(idx, i)) AND the recursive call canBreak(i) is true, return true. State Cleanup: Remember, because we are passing a new index i into the recursive function and not modifying a global string/array, there is no "undo" backtracking required!

The secret to deletion is finding the "Last Safe Node" so you don't accidentally delete nodes shared by other words. As you traverse down the target word, constantly update a temp pointer and reference character (rc) whenever the current node has more than 1 child or its eow is true (indicating an overlap). Once you reach the end of the target word, immediately set its eow to false. Finally, if that last node has 0 children, go back to your temp node and sever the dead branch by setting temp.children[rc - 'a'] = null

To find the shortest unique prefix, upgrade your standard Trie node to include an int freq counter. During the insertion phase, increment this frequency for every single node you step on to track exactly how many words share that specific path. Then, to find the unique prefix for a word, traverse the Trie again and stop the exact moment you land on a node where curr.freq == 1. The substring from the root down to that specific node is mathematically guaranteed to be your shortest unique prefix.

cue: Trie | Top-K ⟩T: Autocomplete / prefix-based top-K suggestions → Trie with a maintained top-K list at every node ⟩M: Build a Trie where each node stores a small sorted list (top5) of (word, weight) pa
oneLiner: To build an optimized Autocomplete system, upgrade the standard Trie node to store an ArrayList of the "Top 5" heavily weighted words. During insertion, at every single character node you step on, add the current word and its weight to that node's list, sort the list by weight descending, and remove the 6th element if the size exceeds 5. During the search phase, traverse down the prefix. If the path breaks, return -1. If you reach the end of the prefix, simply print the cached Top 5 list sitting inside that final node, completely avoiding the need for a costly DFS traversal.

the core engine is treating integers as 32-bit paths (MSB to LSB) in an empty Bitwise Trie, allowing you to make the "Greedy Choice" by always searching for the opposite bit (1 - bit) to guarantee the highest possible value

you don't invent new logic; you capitalize on the rule that XOR is its own inverse by building a prefixXOR array and feeding it directly into your first Trie, with the vital upgrade of dropping "index anchors" at the leaf nodes. When a match is found, you pull those two indices to define your subarray boundaries (Left = min + 1, Right = max), which immediately allows you to filter the results through Scaler's strict three-tier tie-breaker: highest XOR first, then shortest length, and finally the earliest starting index.

exampleProblem: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
oneLiner: The Architecture: Use a recursive function returning a (head, tail) pair to flatten subtrees bottom-up.  The Wiring: Connect them by setting root.right = left_head, then left_tail.right = right_head.  The Trap: Always explicitly sever the left pointer (root.left = null) to prevent invalid tree cycles.  The Edge Case: If a node only has a left child, the true end of that list is the left_tail, not the left_head

The Core Secret (The Bridge): Odd ASCII characters are never adjacent to each other. Evens are never adjacent. If you separate them into two halves, you just need to find one valid pair (a bridge) to connect the Odd half to the Even half.The Trap (Brute Force - $O(N^2)$): Iterating through every single Odd character and comparing it against every single Even character will cause a Time Limit Exceeded (TLE) on massive strings.The HashSet Pivot ($O(N)$ Time, $O(1)$ Space): Since there are only 26 letters, dump characters into two HashSets (Odds and Evens). This reduces the nested loop to a maximum of $13 \times 13 = 169$ constant-time checks.The Masterpiece ($O(N)$ Time, $O(1)$ Space): Ditch the sets. Traverse the string once to find four extremes: minOdd, maxOdd, minEven, and maxEven. Test the 4 cross-combinations. If the absolute difference of any pair is != 1, return true. Otherwise, return false.

The Architecture (Fixed Size): Because any valid permutation of A must be exactly the length of A, the window size never changes. You do not need a while loop to shrink it.The Initial Setup: Create freq_A and freq_B arrays (size 26). Fully populate freq_A, but only populate freq_B with the first A.length() characters of string B. Check if they match using Arrays.equals(); if yes, count++.The Slide ($O(N)$): Loop from i = A.length() to the end of B. Slide the rigid frame by doing exactly two operations:Add Right: Increment the frequency of the new character entering the window: B.charAt(i).Drop Left: Decrement the frequency of the old character falling out of the window: B.charAt(i - A.length()).The Array Index Trap: When dropping the left character, do not accidentally use the raw index math (i - A.length()) inside the frequency array. You must fetch the actual character from string B first, then subtract 'a' to get the correct frequency bucket.

cue: KMP Failure Function ⟩T: Find the period of a string (smallest k such that the string repeats with cycle length k) → build the LPS array on the whole string, then period = length - lps[length-1] ⟩M: B
oneLiner: The Core Concept: The "Period" of a string is the minimum distance k between repeating characters, where $A[i] == A[i \% k]$.The LPS Secret: The last value of the LPS array (LPS[N-1]) tells you the length of the longest prefix that perfectly overlaps with the suffix of the entire string.The Visual Overlap: If a prefix of length $L$ perfectly matches a suffix of length $L$, it means every character in the overlap is exactly $N - L$ steps away from its identical twin.The Golden Formula ($O(N)$): Run KMP to generate the LPS array for the string. The minimum period is always strictly mathematically defined as: $k = N - LPS[N-1]$

That base case bounds swap is the final boss of Edit Distance. Now that you have navigated the 1-indexed string shift and the unique Row 0 / Col 0 initializations, this architecture is fully yours.  Here is your 4-step SRS cheat sheet for the Edit Distance problem. Drop this directly into your notes:  The "Edit Distance Tabulation" SRS Cheat Sheet The State Definition: dp[i][j] represents the minimum number of operations required to convert the first i characters of String A into the first j characters of String B.  The Base Case Trap: Size your table as new int[N+1][M+1]. Unlike LCS, Row 0 and Column 0 cannot be 0.  Row 0: Converting an empty string to String B takes j inserts (dp[0][j] = j).  Col 0: Converting String A to an empty string takes i deletes (dp[i][0] = i).  The Match Transition: Shift your string indices back by one (A.charAt(i-1) == B.charAt(j-1)). If the characters match, no operation is needed. Inherit the cost from the diagonal: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]  The Mismatch Transition: If the characters do not match, you must perform exactly 1 operation. Take the minimum cost of the three possible paths and add 1:  Insert: Look Left (dp[i][j-1])  Delete: Look Up (dp[i-1][j])  Replace: Look Diagonal (dp[i-1][j-1])  dp[i][j] = 1 + Math.min(Replace, Math.min(Insert, Delete))

The Object Mapping: You cannot separate heights and widths. Always map the 2D array into a custom Pair or Envelope object array so they travel together during sorting.  The Timeline Sort: To reduce the 2D problem into a 1D LIS problem, sort the array by one of the dimensions (e.g., Height ascending). This guarantees that any envelope j before envelope i is at least short enough to potentially fit inside i.  The Strict 2D LIS Check: Run the exact LIS nested loops, but your inner if condition must strictly check both dimensions. Equal sizes do not fit inside each other! if (env[j].w < env[i].w && env[j].h < env[i].h)  The Global Max: Just like standard LIS, the biggest nested doll doesn't necessarily end with the very last envelope in the array. Track ans = Math.max(ans, dp[i])

The FAANG Trap: Do not write a 1D recursive function that uses a while loop to check if a string is a palindrome. That creates an $O(N^3)$ algorithm that will hit TLE. You must split the problem into two distinct DP phases to achieve $O(N^2)$.Phase 1: The 2D Palindrome Cache (The Lookup Table): * Use the "Gap Strategy" (diagonal traversal) to build a boolean[][] isPal table.isPal[i][j] is true if the substring from i to j is a palindrome. This gives you $O(1)$ lookups for Phase 2.Phase 2: The 1D Cuts Array (The State Definition): * cuts[i] represents the minimum number of cuts needed to perfectly partition the prefix string from index 0 to i.The Partition Transition:Base Case: If isPal[0][i] == true, the whole prefix is a palindrome. It needs 0 cuts! (cuts[i] = 0).The Cut Loop: If it's not a palindrome, try placing a cut at every index j (from 1 to i).If the right side of the cut is a palindrome (isPal[j][i] == true), then the total cuts = the cuts needed for the left side plus one new cut.cuts[i] = Math.min(cuts[i], cuts[j-1] + 1)

Create a color[] array initialized to -1. 2. Loop through all nodes (to handle disconnected components). If unvisited, paint it 0 and start DFS. 3. The Core Trick: In DFS, paint unvisited neighbors with the opposite color using 1 - col[src]. 4. If a neighbor is already visited and has the same color as the current node (col[nbr] == col[src]), return false!
Cycle Graph Chromatic Number: Even nodes = perfect alternating bipartite (2 colors). Odd nodes = collision at the end (3 colors). return 2 + (A % 2);
Use Colour Grading Bipartite technique using DFS

exampleProblem: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
oneLiner: State Passing: Use a custom Pair(head, tail) to pass the start and end of flattened subtrees upwards.  Traversal: Do a Post-Order traversal (lp = getanswer(left), rp = getanswer(right)).  The Rewire (4 Cases):  Leaf: Return (root, root).  No Left Child: Return (root, right.tail).  No Right Child: Move left to right (root.left = null; root.right = left.head), return (root, left.tail).  Both Children: Connect them! root.left = null, root.right = left.head, left.tail.right = right.head. Return (root, right.tail)

cue: HashMap | ArrayList ⟩T: Design a data structure supporting O(1) insert, remove, and getRandom → HashMap (value → index) + ArrayList (for random access), with swap-to-end-then-pop for removal ⟩M: Maint
oneLiner: Data Structures: You need a HashMap<Value, Index> for $O(1)$ lookup and an ArrayList<Value> for $O(1)$ random access.Insert: Add value to ArrayList, add (Value, ArrayList.size() - 1) to HashMap.The Remove Trick (Swap & Pop): >    * Get the target's index from the HashMap.Overwrite the target's position in the ArrayList with the last element in the ArrayList (list.set(index, lastElement)).Update the HashMap to reflect the last element's new index.Remove the last element from the ArrayList ($O(1)$ time).Remove the target from the HashMap.Random: Use Random.nextInt(list.size()) to pick an index.

cue: Backtracking | Subset Partitioning ⟩T: Partition an array into K subsets, each with equal sum → backtracking, filling one subset completely before moving to the next ⟩M: First check sum % k == 0 — if
exampleProblem: Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets
oneLiner: Prerequisite Check: Total sum must be perfectly divisible by $K$. Each subset target is totalSum / k.Base Case 1 (Global Success): If k == 1, return true. (If $K-1$ buckets are perfectly filled, the remaining unused numbers must naturally sum to the target).Base Case 2 (Bucket Filled): If currsum == targetsum, transition to the next bucket by recursing with idx = 0, currsum = 0, and k - 1.Branching & Pruning: Loop from idx to the end of the array. Only process if visited[i] == false AND currsum + nums[i] <= targetsum.State Reset (Backtrack): Mark visited[i] = true and recurse with idx = i + 1. If the path returns false, undo the choice by setting visited[i] = false and continue the loop

cue: State Machine DP ⟩T: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III (at most 2 transactions) — wrong instinct is to split the array at every index and combine best-left + best-right prefix/suffix DP (O(n) space,
exampleProblem: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
oneLiner: The 4 States: Track firstbuy, firstsell, secondbuy, secondsell.  Initial Values: firstbuy and secondbuy start at -prices[0] (or Integer.MIN_VALUE). Sells start at 0.  The Transitions (Max of doing nothing vs. acting today):  firstbuy = max(firstbuy, -prices[i])  firstsell = max(firstsell, firstbuy + prices[i])  secondbuy = max(secondbuy, firstsell - prices[i])  secondsell = max(secondsell, secondbuy + prices[i])  Pro-Tip: If you update them in reverse order inside the loop (secondsell first, down to firstbuy), you don't even need temporary variables!

Use Two Pointers (i = 0, j = s.length() - 1).  Skip garbage: Use a while loop with !Character.isLetterOrDigit(char) to advance i or decrement j until they hit valid characters.  Compare case-insensitive: Character.toLowerCase(left) != Character.toLowerCase(right).  The Trap to Avoid: Never use s.replaceAll() or s.toLowerCase() on the whole string, as it wastes O(N) space.

The club has a strict maximum capacity of k people.  As you loop through the array, the current number tries to enter the club.  The Check: If the number is already inside the club, you found a duplicate within distance k! Return true.  The Add: Let the number into the club (set.add(nums[i])).  The Kick: If the club is now over capacity (size > k), the bouncer kicks out the person who has been there the longest (nums[i - k]).

cue: Sliding Window ⟩T: Count subarrays with sum/count exactly equal to a target → exactly(K) = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1), sliding window inside the helper ⟩M: Write atMost(goal) as a standard sliding window
oneLiner: Sliding window Exactly K = Atmost(k) - Atmost(k-1)

cue: Sliding Window ⟩T: Circular array, need a sliding window sum that wraps around → maintain a running window sum, slide with modular indices instead of recomputing each time ⟩M: k=0 as a trivial all-zer
oneLiner: Front (Prompt):Problem: Defuse the Bomb (Circular Array Sliding Window)Goal: Replace each element with the sum of the next k elements (or previous if k < 0).The Trap: $O(N \cdot K)$ nested loops for every element.Back (Key Insights & Logic):Core Strategy: Calculate the first window once, then Slide in $O(N)$.The Secret Weapon: Modulo arithmetic (% n) perfectly simulates a circular array without needing to duplicate it.The Blueprint:Handle k == 0: Return empty array of 0s immediately.Set Initial Boundaries:If k > 0: start = 1, end = kIf k < 0: start = n - Math.abs(k), end = n - 1Calculate Initial Sum: Loop from start to end to get the first windowSum.Slide the Window (Loop i from 0 to n-1):ans[i] = windowSum;Subtract Outgoing: windowSum -= code[start];Add Incoming: windowSum += code[(end + 1) % n]; (Use end + 1 because we need the element just outside our current window!)Move Pointers: start = (start + 1) % n; and end = (end + 1) % n;

cue: Sliding Window ⟩T: You see a problem asking for subarrays with "exactly \(K\) odd/specific items", which tells you to transform it into finding "at most \(K\)" minus "at most \(K-1\)" ⟩M: You immediat
oneLiner: You immediately run a sliding window where the right pointer expands to count items, and the left pointer shrinks the window the moment your count exceeds \(K\)

cue: Sliding Window ⟩T: You need to count how many existing unique numbers fit inside the range [L, L + n - 1]. ⟩M: Deduplicate and sort the array, then use a sliding window to find the densest cluster of
exampleProblem: Minimum Number of Operations to Make Array Continuous
oneLiner: To make an array continuous, first remove all duplicates and sort the remaining unique elements in ascending order, keeping track of the original array length (n). Next, use a sliding window to treat each unique number as the starting element (L) of a target range that must end at L + n - 1. By expanding a right pointer to count how many existing unique numbers fit inside this valid window, you determine how many elements you can keep; subtracting this count from the original length (n) gives the operations needed, and the minimum value found across all windows is your final answer.

cue: Linear Scan ⟩T: "Longest run of X in a linear scan" — wrong instinct is a sliding window with two pointers, or forgetting the final flush so a run ending at the last index gets dropped. ⟩M: Single pas
oneLiner: ame skeleton solves "longest run of 0s", "max consecutive ones with at most K flips" (→ that one does become sliding window — worth a separate card as the contrast)maxone as Integer.MIN_VALUE works here but returns a garbage-adjacent value on an all-zeros array only by luck (it gets flushed to 0). Initialize to 0 instead — cleaner and correct by construction.

cue: Deque ⟩T: Longest subarray constrained by max - min of the whole window — wrong instinct is comparing only the two endpoints |nums[left] - nums[right]|, or tracking length with a separate counter that
exampleProblem: Longest Continuous Subarray With Absolute Diff Less Than or Equal to Limit
oneLiner: Topic: Sliding Window + Monotonic Deque Sibling cards: LC 239 (Sliding Window Maximum — one deque, fixed window) → LC 1438 (two deques, variable window). 239 is the prerequisite; if 1438 feels hard, the gap is 239. Contrast card: LC 485 (Max Consecutive Ones) — looks similar, is not a window; that's the "running counter" pattern. The tell: 485 has no shrink condition. Trap: stale windowmax/windowmin locals computed before the shrink loop → infinite loop. Always read the deque front inside the while condition. Alt approach worth one line in notes: two TreeMaps (or a multiset) gives the same O(n log n) — deque is the O(n) answer and the one interviewers want.

cue: Monotonic Queue (Deque) ⟩T: Window-max over all size-k windows; wrong instinct is a max-heap (O(n log n) and you can't cheaply evict the element that slid out). Reach for a monotonic decreasing deque
oneLiner: Use a Deque<Integer> storing indices, kept monotonic decreasing by value (front = largest, back = smallest). Iterate i from 0 to nums.length - 1:  Evict expired front: if deque.peekFirst() == i - k, that index has slid out of the window → pollFirst(). (Front can only ever be one element too old, so an if suffices, not a while.) Maintain monotonicity: while the deque isn't empty and nums[deque.peekLast()] <= nums[i], pollLast(). Those smaller-or-equal older elements can never be the max while i is in the window. Add current: deque.offerLast(i). Record answer: once i >= k - 1 (first full window complete), the max is nums[deque.peekFirst()] → write to ans[i - k + 1].  Sizing: ans = new int[nums.length - k + 1].
